coredata.dc:title: Unlocking the potential of eggplant peel: Powerful peroxidase nanozyme for l-cysteine determination in pharmaceutical supplements
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Elshenawy, Eman A.
Shaldam, Moataz A.
coredata.dc:description: For the first time, eggplant peel biowaste was upcycled through a very fast one-pot microwave synthesis to produce cost-effective and environmentally friendly blue-fluorescent carbon dots (EPE-CDs). The resulting EPE-CDs emitted fluorescence at 465 nm when excited at 400 nm and exhibited enhanced peroxidase-like behavior without metal doping requirement. They were able to oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to generate 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) leading to a new emission peak at 560 nm. Through the inner filter effect, DAP was able to quench the fluorescence of EPE-CDs, causing a change from colourless to yellow. By leveraging the fluorescence characteristics of EPE-CDs and DAP, dual-emission platform was established. This platform could be used for the catalytic detection of H2O2 within the 10–110 μM range. The introduction of L-Cysteine, containing reductive thiols that hinder DAP production, resulted in a reduction in the fluorescence intensity of DAP. The study demonstrated that fluorescence intensities ratio 465 nm / 560 nm (I465/I560) exhibited a linear relationship with L-Cysteine concentration between 4 and 60 μM, reaching detection limit as low as 0.69 μM. Furthermore, when applying the ratiometric fluorescence platform to quantify L-Cysteine in actual samples, the average recoveries ranged from 99.50 % to 101.40 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) not exceeding 2 %. Through the exploration of the fluorescence properties and nanozyme-like capabilities, this research offers a novel perspective on utilizing biowaste sources for CD synthesis and utilization of these CDs as nanozymes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biowaste-derived carbon dots
Carbon dot nanozyme
Peroxidase mimicking activity
Ratiometric sensor
L-Cysteine
Greenness assessment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of eggplant peel extract carbon dots (EPE-CDs)
Ratiometric fluorescence determination of H2O2 and l-cysteine
Analysis of l-cysteine in real samples
Synthesis, characterization, and optical properties of EPE-CDs
Kinetics and catalytic mechanism of peroxidase-like activity of EPE-CDs
Ratiometric assay of H2O2 and Cys
Determination of Cys in real samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the EPE-CDs, (b) HRTEM of the EPE-CDs; inset: lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, (c) FTIR spectrum of EPE-CDs and (d) XPS analysis of EPE-CDs.
(a) UV, excitation, and emission spectra of EPE-CDs and (b) emission spectra of EPE-CDs with varying excitation wavelengths from 330 nm to 430 nm.
(a) Fluorescence spectra and (b) UV–vis spectra of EPE-CDs in presence of different components in the sensing system.

Fluorescence changes as a function of reaction time for the sensor (a) at various OPD levels and (b) the corresponding Lineweaver-Burk plot, and (c) at different H2O2 levels and (d) the corresponding Lineweaver-Burk plot.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the ratiometric sensor with different H2O2 concentrations, (b) the linear relationship between I560/I465 and H2O2 concentrations, (c) fluorescence emission spectra of the ratiometric sensor with different Cys concentrations (inset: Corresponding color change under UV light) (d) the linear relationship between I465/I560 and Cys concentrations.
Response of the ratiometric sensor towards Cys and possible interferants.
Complex GAPI assessment of the developed ratiometric sensor for Cys determination.
Design of ratiometric fluorescent sensor for determination of H2O2 and Cys.
Scheme depicting the synthesis process of EPE-CDs sensor.

head.ce:title: Unlocking the potential of eggplant peel: Powerful peroxidase nanozyme for l-cysteine determination in pharmaceutical supplements

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy-Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy-Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy- Kafrelsheikh University, Kafrelsheikh 33516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy- Kafrelsheikh University, Kafrelsheikh 33516, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the first time, eggplant peel biowaste was upcycled through a very fast one-pot microwave synthesis to produce cost-effective and environmentally friendly blue-fluorescent carbon dots (EPE-CDs). The resulting EPE-CDs emitted fluorescence at 465 nm when excited at 400 nm and exhibited enhanced peroxidase-like behavior without metal doping requirement. They were able to oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to generate 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) leading to a new emission peak at 560 nm. Through the inner filter effect, DAP was able to quench the fluorescence of EPE-CDs, causing a change from colourless to yellow. By leveraging the fluorescence characteristics of EPE-CDs and DAP, dual-emission platform was established. This platform could be used for the catalytic detection of H2O2 within the 10–110 μM range. The introduction of L-Cysteine, containing reductive thiols that hinder DAP production, resulted in a reduction in the fluorescence intensity of DAP. The study demonstrated that fluorescence intensities ratio 465 nm / 560 nm (I465/I560) exhibited a linear relationship with L-Cysteine concentration between 4 and 60 μM, reaching detection limit as low as 0.69 μM. Furthermore, when applying the ratiometric fluorescence platform to quantify L-Cysteine in actual samples, the average recoveries ranged from 99.50 % to 101.40 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) not exceeding 2 %. Through the exploration of the fluorescence properties and nanozyme-like capabilities, this research offers a novel perspective on utilizing biowaste sources for CD synthesis and utilization of these CDs as nanozymes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As effective biocatalysts, natural enzymes are a part of several physiological processes and reactions in multicellular organisms [1]. Nevertheless, a number of disadvantages with natural enzymes seriously impede their development and use; they include their instability (easy denature), high cost, and excessive sensitivity to environmental factors that impact their catalytic activity [2–4]. Consequently, artificial enzymes especially when paired with nanotechnology have surfaced as stable and affordable substitutes for natural enzymes that may overcome these limitations and maintain great catalytic activity [5]. Nanozymes that comprise a variety of synthetic nanomaterials with intrinsic enzyme-mimic features have attracted a lot of interest. Nanozymes are advantageous over natural enzymes owing to their inexpensive cost, vast production scale, and exceptional catalytic activity [6,7].

Numerous nanomaterials were created with the goal of achieving high levels of enzyme-like activity through the integration of multivalent elements into nanostructures or the imitation of real enzyme active centers [8,9]. It has been demonstrated that carbon nanomaterials with distinct geometric, optical, thermal, electrical, and mechanical properties can imitate the catalytic functions of natural enzymes and present a viable biological enzyme substitute [10]. Carbon dots (CDs), as the most varied growing class of carbon nanomaterials, have gained widespread recognition because of their exceptional photophysical properties, minimal cytotoxicity, affordable precursors, stability, cost-effective synthesis, and remarkable catalytic properties [11–14]. The biggest benefit of CDs nanozymes over semiconductor nanoparticles and organometallic nanozymes is their environmental friendliness [15].

Biowastes as sustainable and natural resources have been deemed appealing starting materials, to produce biocompatible and biodegradable CDs, because of their affordability, low cost, abundance, and ecologically friendly qualities [16]. The production of CDs from biowaste upcycling enables the conversion of this low-value waste into valuable products [17,18]. There have been some reports that indicated the production of CDs with peroxidase activity utilizing waste materials or non-chemical sources [19–21]. Eggplant peel is typically discarded as waste with an enormous amount ends up in the garbage each year, ignoring its potential benefits [22]. Leftover eggplant peels are rich in several nutrients and elements that can be doped into the finished CDs, making them one of the best precursors for the preparation of CDs. Among these elements are transition metals that can boost CDs’ catalytic activity and structural stability [23].

L-cysteine (Cys) is a crucial source of sulfur in human metabolism. While it is categorized as a non-essential amino acid, infants, the elderly, and those with specific metabolic diseases or malabsorption disorders may require Cys. It possesses antioxidant qualities and may undergo redox reactions, which makes it an essential component of physiological processes [24,25]. A lack of Cys or an aberrant level of the protein will result in several illnesses, including diabetes, heart disease, improper liver metabolic function, different skin or hair issues, etc [26–29]. Several methods, including electrochemical methods [30], fluorescence methods [31,32], colorimetric detection [33,34], and chromatographic methods [35] have been reported for determining Cys. Among these methods, the ratiometric fluorescence methods make use of a dual emission system's fluorescence intensity ratio as a sensing probe in order to lessen background interference and improve the accuracy of the results [32]. As such, the ratiometric fluorescence approach is particularly attractive. However, to the best of our knowledge, no research on the ratiometric fluorescence detection of Cys utilizing CDs nanozyme produced from biowaste has been published.

This work describes the preparation of novel biowaste-derived CDs from eggplant peel as precursor through a facile and eco-friendly microwave-assisted approach. The prepared CDs exhibited high peroxidase-mimic activities in existence of H2O2 without any metal doping, which could catalyze oxidization of colorless o-phenylenediamine (OPD) in just five minutes and generate the yellow 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with a fluorescence emission at 560 nm. As a result, the system of CDs and DAP had two emission peaks, however, inner filter effect of DAP reduced CDs' fluorescence at 465 nm (Scheme 1 ). Because the peroxidase-like activity of CDs could be influenced by H2O2 concentration, the fluorescence intensity of CDs and DAP varied with H2O2 levels. With the addition of the antioxidant Cys, the FL intensity at 560 nm will decrease and the peak at 465 nm will increase. On the basis of these, novel ratiometric fluorescent platform aimed at determination of H2O2 and Cys was developed.

o-Phenylenediamine (OPD) and sodium azide were obtained from SD Fine Chemicals, India. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 50 %), mercuric chloride and urea were obtained from LANXESS, Spain. L-Cysteine hydrochloride (purity, 98 %) was bought from Acros Organics, Belgium. L-glutamic acid monosodium and t-butanol were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. p-benzoquinone and ascorbic acid were purchased from CDH fine chemical, India. Quinine Sulfate was procured from Alpha Chemika, India. Glutathione was obtained from Solarbio Life Sciences, China. Phosphoric acid, potassium phosphate monobasic, sodium hydroxide, lactose anhydrous, fructose, maltose, sucrose, glucose, KCl, and NaCl were bought from ADWIC company, Egypt. Pantogar® hard gelatin capsules containing 20 mg L-Cysteine per capsule (Batch No. 2322300) and acetylcistein® sachets (Batch No. 1023904) were purchased from a local pharmacy.

JASCO FP-6300 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan) was used for the fluorescence measurements. During the CD preparation process, household microwave oven (1000 W) were used. JEOL JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (Japan) operating at 200 kV accelerating voltage was used to perform energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). Using Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Japan), UV absorption spectra were obtained. Using a Jasco FT/IR-4000 spectrophotometer (USA), FTIR plots were obtained. XPS measurements were conducted using a K-ALPHA instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The XPS analysis was performed with monochromatic X-ray Al K-alpha radiation, ranging from −10 to 1350 eV, and a spot size of 400 µM. The measurements were carried out under a pressure of 10-9 mbar. The full spectrum pass energy was set at 200 eV, while the narrow spectrum pass energy was set at 50 eV. The analysis of the zeta potential was conducted by employing Brookhaven zeta potential analyzer (USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of eggplant peel extract carbon dots (EPE-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: First, eggplants were bought at a nearby market, cleaned with distilled water, and peeled. The peels were then allowed to dry naturally before being ground in a grinder. One gram of the resulting powder was extracted for one hour in the dark using 10 mL of distilled water containing 2 % acetic acid. After that, 5 mL of the extract was heated for 2.5 min in a household microwave till obtaining a light brown residue. Resultant residue was cooled to room temperature prior dissolving in ten mL of distilled water, sonicated for 15 min, centrifuged for 20 min at 6000 rpm, and filtered through a 0.22 µm syringe filter. The prepared EPE-CDs aqueous solution was stable during storage at 4 °C for two months. In order to ascertain the concentration of the synthesized EPE-CDs, the resultant solution underwent freeze-drying to obtain the CDs powder, yielding a concentration of 10 mg/mL for the EPE-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence determination of H2O2 and l-cysteine
ce:section.ce:para: To detect H2O2, 0.5 mL of phosphate buffer (50 mM, pH 6.0) was mixed with various volumes of H2O2 (3.3 mM) and 100 μL of OPD (3.6 mM). After that, 100 μL of EPE-CDs (10 mg/mL) were added, then, using distilled water, total volume was brought to 3 mL. This solution was held warm at 50 ˚C about 5 min. Fluorescence emission intensities were recorded at 465 nm and 560 nm after exciting at 400 nm.

In order to test Cys, 0.5 mL of phosphate buffer (50 mM, pH 6.0) was mixed with 100 μL of H2O2 (3.3 mM) and various volumes of Cys (0.3 mM). Then 100 μL of OPD (3.6 mM). and 100 μL of EPE-CDs (10 mg/mL) were added, then, using distilled water, total volume was brought to 3 mL. This solution was held warm at 50 ˚C about 5 min. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded the same as above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of l-cysteine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The contents of ten capsules of pantogar® or ten sachets of acetylcistein® were finely powdered and thoroughly combined. Accurately weighed portions equivalent to 45.5 mg of Cys were then placed in a 50 mL volumetric flask, dissolved in 25 mL of distilled water, sonicated for 30 min, completed to the mark with the same solvent, and thoroughly mixed. After filtering the solution through a 0.45 µm filter, 1 mL of the filtrate was diluted to 25 mL with distilled water. A 200 µL volume of the resultant solution was measured using Cys determination procedure described in section 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, characterization, and optical properties of EPE-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: EPE-CDs were prepared in 2.5 min using a one-step microwave pyrolysis process on leftover eggplant peel (Scheme 2 ). TEM measurements were used to characterize the morphology of the prepared EPE-CDs (Fig. 1 a). EPE-CDs had a uniform spherical shape, good dispersibility, and particle sizes between 6–9 nm. EPE-CDs showed regular lattice edges having 0.23 nm spacing, that can be related to graphene (100) crystal planes [36], as shown in the HRTEM image (Fig. 1b). The surface composition of the EPE-CDs was analyzed using FT-IR (Fig. 1c). Acquired spectra showed stretching vibrations (cm−1) of C-O (1080), C-N (1383), C = C (1629), C = O (1659), and O-H/NH (3433). Additionally, multiple small absorption peaks were observed in the spectrum, possibly corresponding to O-Co-O and N-Co-N bonds within metallic sensitivity range of 1044, 800–470 cm−1 [37]. To further investigate the chemical makeup of the EPE-CDs, XPS analysis was conducted (Fig. 1d). The EPE-CDs spectrum exhibited peaks at C1s (285.2 eV), N1s (399.9 eV), O1s (532.1 eV), and Co2p (791.2 eV). Based on these peaks, the composition of the EPE-CDs was calculated to be 66.5 % carbon, 27.5 % oxygen, 3.9 % nitrogen, and 2.1 % cobalt. The C1s spectra (Fig. S1) revealed binding energies of 288 eV, 286.2 eV, and 284.5 eV, corresponding to C = O, C-O/C-N, and C = C bonds, respectively. In the O1s spectra, three prominent peaks were observed at 531, 532.5, and 533.1 eV, representing O-C = O, C = O, and C-OH bonds, respectively. The N1s spectra displayed two prominent peaks at 399.1 eV and 401.2 eV, indicating the presence of amidic N and N of amine bonds. The Co2p spectrum exhibited eight peaks ranging from 777 eV to 888 eV, suggesting the existence of Co atoms in various oxidation states, including + 2, +3, alongside with Co-N bond.

The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of EPE–CDs were used to study their photophysical characteristics. Fig. 2 a illustrates the detection of two peaks at 242 nm and 284 nm, recognized for transition π → π* of C = C in sp2 domains and one peak at 328 nm associated with the transition n → π* of C = O group. Under UV light, EPE-CDs generated blue fluorescence that showed stable behavior over two-months duration. EPE-CDs demonstrated an excitation-independent emission feature, as demonstrated by the nearly constant position of the fluorescence peak at 465 nm when the excitation wavelength was varied between 330 and 430 nm (Fig. 2b). The highest fluorescence intensity was recorded after excitation at 400 nm. Using quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54) as a reference, the fluorescence quantum yield of EPE-CDs in water was determined to be 5.2 %.

To investigate peroxidase-mimic activities of EPE-CDs, OPD was chosen as the peroxidase substrate. EPE-CDs catalyze oxidation of nonfluorescent OPD to produce yellow fluorescence peak at 560 nm of DAP with existence of H2O2, as shown in Fig. 3 a. Conversely, the control groups exhibited nearly no fluorescence signal when H2O2 or EPE-CDs were absent. A solution containing the complete system of EPE-CDs/H2O2/OPD displayed yellow color possessing prominent 430 nm absorption band (Fig. 3b). Without H2O2 or EPE-CDs, on the other hand, the reaction system's color was nearly colorless. These outcomes collectively verified that EPE-CDs possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity.

Moreover, pH and temperature were found to have an impact on the catalytic activity of EPE-CDs. The impact of these two parameters on the catalytic relative activity of OPD oxidation is seen in Fig. S2a and b. The relative activity (%) of an enzyme is calculated by A1/A2 × 100 %, where A1 and A2 stand for the sample's absorbance and the maximum absorbance, respectively. By altering the pH between 3 and 9, the highest peroxidase-like activity of EPE-CDs was obtained at pH 6.0. Temperature variations between 20 °C and 70 °C were used to track the peroxidase-like activity of EPE-CDs, with 50 °C providing the highest catalytic activity. Furthermore, the experimental findings in Fig. S2c demonstrate that the catalytic reaction takes five minutes to reach its maximal activity. The effect of OPD and H2O2 concentrations on the catalytic activity was also studied (Fig. S2d and e). For this system, 110 μM of H2O2 and 120 μM of OPD were the ideal concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics and catalytic mechanism of peroxidase-like activity of EPE-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the features of the peroxidase-like activity of EPE-CDs using OPD and H2O2 as substrates, steady-state kinetic analysis was performed (Fig. 4 ). For kinetics of OPD oxidation by EPE-CDs, the concentration of OPD was varied while maintaining a constant H2O2 concentration of 110 μM. While for the kinetics of H2O2 determination, a concentration of 120 μM OPD was employed with various H2O2 concentrations. The Lineweaver-Burk plot was employed to find the kinetic data, maximum velocity (Vmax), and Michaelis-Menten constant (Km). The Lineweaver-Burk equation is stated as follows:
1 / V =
K m
/
V
max
×
1 /
S
+ 1 /
V
max
where [S] “substrate concentration (H2O2 or OPD)”, Vmax and V “ maximal” and “initial” reaction velocities, respectively, and Km “Michaelis-Menten constant”. Values of Km and Vmax for EPE-CDs toward OPD were found to be 0.25 mM and 8.95 × 10-8 MS-1, respectively. Conversely, toward H2O2, Km and Vmax were 0.07 mM and 4.80 × 10-8 MS-1, respectively. EPE-CDs have a significantly lower Km values towards H2O2 and OPD compared to horseradish peroxidase (0.34 mM and 0.59 mM [38], respectively), indicating that the produced EPE-CDs have a strong affinity for H2O2 or OPD. In addition, the large value of Vmax suggested that EPE-CDs had a high catalytic activity.

The capture of free radicals produced during the catalytic process was examined in order to explore the catalytic mechanism of the EPE-CDs nanozyme. The radical scavengers selected were tert-butanol, sodium azide, and p-benzoquinone, for hydroxyl radical (•OH), singlet oxygen (1O2), and the superoxide anion radical (•O2 −), respectively. The system was then exposed to one of the three previously indicated free radical scavengers (100 μM for sodium azide and p-benzoquinone, and 100 μg/mL for tert-butanol), and the fluorescence intensity value of the solution at 560 nm was measured. As can be seen in Fig. S3a, the system's fluorescence intensity was unaffected by p-benzoquinone and tert-butanol, but it decreased when sodium azide was present. These findings indicated that the EPE-CDs' catalytic mechanism involved the production of 1O2.

When H2O2 is present, EPE-CDs can oxidize OPD to create DAP, which has an emission wavelength of 560 nm. This results in a dual-emission system with two emission peaks: one at 465 nm from EPE-CDs and one at 560 nm from DAP. Nevertheless, formation of DAP greatly reduced fluorescence of CDs. The absorption spectrum of DAP showed a clear overlap with the emission and excitation spectra of CDs, as shown in Fig. S3b. This indicated that DAP could quench CD fluorescence through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE). Zeta potential was measured for both DAP and EPE-CDs. They displayed, respectively, zeta potentials of −24.0 mV and –32.6 mV (Fig. S3c). EPE-CDs and DAP would be farther distant than 10 nm since both Zeta potentials were negative and there was electrostatic repulsion [39]. As a result, IFE is the possible reason for DAP's quenching of EPE-CDs. To explore the existence of additional complementary mechanisms alongside IFE, the Stern-Volmer equation was utilized to elucidate the potential mechanism:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
Q
Here, F and F0 denote the fluorescence intensities of EPE-CDs in the presence and absence of DAP, respectively, while [Q] represents the concentration of DAP, and KSV signifies the Stern-Volmer quenching constant [40]. The fluorescence quenching efficiencies (F0 /F) were examined at three different temperatures (Fig. S4). It was observed that KSV remained constant with rising temperature, with values of 8.13 × 104, 8.02 × 104, and 8.02 × 104 L mol−1 at 298, 313, and 333 K, respectively, indicating the absence of both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms. Furthermore, the UV spectrum of the mixture of EPE-CDs and DAP was found to be the sum of the UV spectra of the individual components, as illustrated in Fig. S5, with no new material formed. This was corroborated by the fact that the spectrum of the mixture corresponded to that of DAP alone when the same concentration of EPE-CDs was used as a blank, thereby ruling out the possibility of a static quenching mechanism [41,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric assay of H2O2 and Cys
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimum conditions, the sensor's ability to measure H2O2 and Cys was investigated. The fluorescence of the system shifted from blue to yellow with increasing H2O2 concentration. This resulted in an increase in emission intensity at 560 nm and a progressive drop at 465 nm (Fig. 5 a). Meanwhile, the colour of the solution gradually changed from colourless to a yellow that was visible in daylight. With a linear regression equation of y = 0.033 x + 0.587 (R2 = 0.997), the fluorescence intensity ratio (I560/I465) showed linear correlation with the relevant H2O2 concentrations between 10 and 110 μM (Fig. 5b). The detection limit (DL) was determined to be 1.79 μM based on 3.3 σ/s (where σ was the standard deviation and s was the slope of the curve).

The developed ratiometric sensor can be extended to determineCys as it can inhibit the production of DAP. When Cys is added to the EPE-CDs/H2O2/OPD system, fluorescence intensities at 560 nm (I560) significantly decreases while at 465 nm (I465) increases (Fig. 5c). A regression equation of y = 0.039 x + 0.649 (R2 = 0.996) illustrates the linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I465/I560) and the corresponding Cys concentrations between 4 and 60 μM reaching a DL of 0.69 μM (Fig. 5d).

Furthermore, the effect of multiple potential interferents was investigated individually in order to assess the selectivity of the suggested sensor for Cys quantification in real samples. The results shown in Fig. 6 show that the ratios I465/I560 are nearly the same as the blank in the presence of interfering elements, and that I465/I560 greatly increased only when Cys was present. This indicates that EPE-CDs/ H2O2/OPD has great selectivity because it can detect Cys even in the presence of other potential interfering sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cys in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For practicality validation, Cys detection was done in real samples. Cys content was determined in pharmaceutical samples by the suggested probe. The procedures outlined in section 2.5 were followed in the preparation and testing of the samples. Developed probe was effectively applied for Cys determination with mean %recoveries of 101.4 % and 99.5 % for the two formulations (Table 1 ). %RSD of all samples was no more than 2 % indicating that the EPE-CDs/ H2O2/OPD ratiometric probe was reliable for Cys detection.

There is a growing awareness of the need to green the analytical techniques currently in use. As a result, a number of solutions based on reagent reduction, solvent depletion, waste minimization and recycling, and passivation and degradation of toxic wastes have been put forth [43]. Many metrics have been produced to measure the greenness of analytical procedures. A recent metric to evaluate greenness of analytical methods is the ComplexGAPI assessment, which is an upgrade to the widely used GAPI metric [44]. Five pentagrams are used to depict this assessment, which offers a thorough analysis of the complete approach, starting with sample preparation and collection, solvents and chemicals used, equipment, energy use and waste generated. The additional hexagonal structure at the assessment's base reflects the pre-analysis steps. This assessment uses a color-coded approach; yellow and green represent medium and low risk, respectively, and red denotes a considerable risk to the environment. With six green regions, our proposed method demonstrated good greenness (Fig. 7 ). Seven green zones in the additional hexagonal form assessing the pre-analysis procedure further demonstrated the environmentally friendly nature of the EPE-CDs probe preparation process.

EPE-CDs derived from biowaste were successfully synthesized and exhibited peroxidase-mimic activities and fluorescence features. A detection platform based on ratiometric fluorescence for H2O2 and L-Cysteine was developed using CDs catalysis of OPD oxidation, which proved to be rapid and accurate. This method offers several advantages, including its low cost, environmentally friendly nature, and convenience. It allows for the conversion of low value waste into high value products and does not involve complicated procedures neither in EPE-CDs preparation nor in process of detection. Platform’s fluorescence intensities were affected by increasing the concentration of H2O2 in a concentration-dependent manner, with a detection limit of 1.79 μM. Moreover, this method demonstrated high sensitivity and good selectivity for the determination of L-Cysteine, with a detection limit of 0.69 μM. The proposed method has also been successfully applied for the determination of L-Cysteine in pharmaceutical supplements.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Eman A. Elshenawy: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Moataz A. Shaldam: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111933.

ce:section.ce:para:

Biotechnol Appl Biochem

Particuology
